page: UpCloud 4xCPU-8GB is a general-purpose virtual server hosted on the KVM hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an AMD EPYC 7542 processor with 4 shared vCPUs operating at a base frequency of 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 8.0 GB of system memory, yielding 2.0 GB of RAM per core, alongside a bundled 160 GB local SSD for storage. A single complimentary public IPv4 address is included.

Performance benchmarks indicate moderate capabilities with some resource constraints. Single-core CPU performance via stress-ng falls in the bottom 10%, while multi-core performance is positioned in the bottom 25%. Memory bandwidth tests show average performance for cached small-block reads at 387.96 GB/sec and large-block writes at 26.35 GB/sec, though large-block uncached reads drop to a weaker bottom 25% tier. Cryptographic and integer operations perform at an average level, supported by hardware-accelerated instruction sets such as AES and SHA_NI.

The server's shared CPU allocation presents a tradeoff, making it highly cost-efficient for workloads that do not require sustained, dedicated compute power. Database operations and basic text processing perform at average levels, while web serving throughput and Redis SET operations are relatively weak. This profile is best suited for development environments, lightweight web serving, basic background task processing, and low-intensity database workloads.
description: UpCloud 4xCPU-8GB is a general-purpose virtual server utilizing the KVM hypervisor and an x86_64 AMD EPYC 7542 processor. It is configured with 4 shared vCPUs running at 2.0 GHz, 8.0 GB of system memory, and a bundled 160 GB local SSD. Benchmark data reveals average performance in memory bandwidth and cryptographic operations, alongside weaker outcomes in single-core and multi-core CPU stress tests. The shared CPU allocation offers a cost-effective resource profile for non-intensive workloads. This server is best suited for development environments, lightweight web serving, and low-demand database applications.
